points: Poverty is a multidimensional challenge involving hunger, lack of shelter, inadequate healthcare and education, absence of clean water and sanitation, and a sense of helplessness—illustrated through the cases of Ram Saran (urban daily-wage labourer) and Lakha Singh (rural landless labourer).

Social scientists analyze poverty using multiple indicators; the National Multidimensional Poverty Index (NMPI) measures poverty across 12 indicators grouped into health (nutrition, mortality, maternal health), education (schooling, attendance), and standard of living (cooking fuel, sanitation, water, housing, electricity, assets, bank account).

Key concepts include social exclusion (denial of equal opportunities, as seen in the caste system) and vulnerability (greater probability of certain groups becoming or remaining poor due to limited assets and risk-handling capacity).

The poverty line is the minimum income or consumption needed for basic needs, varying by time and place; in India, traditional measurement used calorie requirements (2400 calories in rural areas, 2100 in urban areas), now complemented by the Multidimensional Poverty Index which directly measures deprivations.

Consumption-based poverty in India declined from 45% in 1993-94 to 22% in 2011-12, while multidimensional poverty fell from 25% in 2015-16 to 15% in 2019-21, with 13.5 crore people escaping poverty during 2015-2021.

Significant inter-state disparities exist—Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Punjab, and Maharashtra have HCR below 10%, while states like Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, and Rajasthan have shown improvement but higher poverty rates; different states adopted varied approaches (Kerala focused on human development, West Bengal on land reforms, Andhra Pradesh on PDS).

Most vulnerable social groups are Scheduled Tribes (43% poor), Scheduled Castes (29% poor), and among economic groups, rural agricultural labourers (34% poor) and urban casual labourers (34% poor); within poor families, women, elderly, and female infants often face greater deprivation.

Globally, extreme poverty (below $2.15 per day) fell from 16.27% in 2010 to 9.05% in 2019; China achieved 0.1% poverty (2020), South Asia reduced poverty from 13% to 11% (2017-2021), while Sub-Saharan Africa remains the region with highest concentration of poor at 35%.

Historical causes include low economic development under British colonial rule that ruined traditional handicrafts and discouraged industries, resulting in few job opportunities and low income growth until the 1980s.

Economic causes include unequal distribution of land and resources, ineffective implementation of land reforms, limited impact of Green Revolution to select regions, and insufficient industrial job creation leading to growth of informal sector employment.

Socio-cultural factors include high expenditure on social obligations and religious ceremonies, while indebtedness traps small farmers who borrow at high risk—high indebtedness is both a cause and effect of poverty.
Anti-poverty strategy is based on promoting economic growth (which has created a strong link between growth and poverty reduction since the 1980s) and targeted anti-poverty programmes.

Key programmes include MGNREGA (100 days wage employment with one-third reservation for women), PM Poshan (school nutrition to improve health and enrolment), PM Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (antenatal care to reduce maternal/infant mortality), and PM Ujjwala Yojana (free LPG connections to poor households, issued in women's names, promoting health and environment).

Despite progress, poverty reduction remains a compelling challenge with rural-urban and inter-state disparities; the concept of poverty is broadening from income-based definitions to human poverty, which considers education, shelter, healthcare, job security, self-confidence, and freedom from discrimination.
